result:
I keep thinking about the moment a word becomes a thing. Not metaphorically, but actually: a sound you shape with your tongue and lips, then—pop—there it is, loosed into the room like a sparrow that might choose to perch on the lampshade or slam itself against the windowpane until you fling the sash open and let night air rush in. Once the word leaves, you can’t invoice it for damages. It does what it wants.

I used to believe that if I could just install the right sentence in the right skull, the mechanism of that skull would whirr, click, and then—presto—understanding. I spent years crafting sentences the way watchmakers assemble escapements, every gear polished, every tooth angled. I’d hand them over, confident. Months later I’d bump into evidence that the sentence had been pocketed, bent, spent on something I never intended. The word become coin, the coin become bottle cap, the bottle cap become something a toddler chokes on. You can’t un-coin a sentence. You can’t crawl down someone’s throat and retrieve it.

So I started to wonder where words go after they fall out of us. Do they evaporate like the jets of water in those desert-cooler diagrams, rising until they condense into a private cloud that follows one person around, raining on their commute decades later? Or do they drift sideways into a library invisible to the living, a Borgesian branch whose stacks are crowded with every syllable ever uttered, each one wearing the residue of the voice that launched it. The shouts are bound in scarlet leather; the bedtime whispers, pale suede that smells of talcum and milk. Somewhere in that library sits the sentence I hissed at my brother in 1993—You wish you were me—now stretched on a shelf between a 14th-century nun’s ecstatic Latin and a TikTok catchphrase that will die before Christmas. Every night a blind librarian (who is perhaps also me, or you) runs a fingertip across the spines, deciding which volume to open, which echo to let out for a breath of darkness.

I keep dreaming of a house I’ve never lived in. It has a green bakelite phone that rings though the line was severed decades ago. When I pick up, a voice recites a weather report for a city that doesn’t exist: “Light snow at dawn, turning to aluminum by noon.” I wake with the taste of metal on my tongue and the conviction that the house is where all my misplaced words retire. They sit in rocking chairs, swapping stories about the mouths they once toured. “He bit me so hard I still have dental indentations,” confesses the adjective audacious. “She let me dangle at the end of a prayer like a chandelier,” sighs nevertheless. They vote on whether to let new arrivals stay; some syllables are too bruised and get sent back to haunt elementary-school spelling bees. The house creaks because every board is made of consonants that dried too quickly. Vowels are the curtains—bright, restless, impossible to hem.

Sometimes I try to visit while awake. I close my eyes and picture the front path, but the address keeps sliding. 1426 Orbit Drive becomes 2614 Gravity Loop; numbers pirouette like skating chameleons. Scholars of lucid dreaming advise planting a seed: decide what hand you’ll find in your pocket, they say, and when you see that impossible hand, you’ll know you’re inside the dream. I choose a sixth finger wearing a copper ring engraved with the word nevertheless. Yet every time I reach, I feel only the reassuring five. Perhaps the house doesn’t want an owner anymore; it wants tenancy in me. Perhaps I am the foyer where these footsore syllables collapse.

What would happen if one morning language simply refused service? Not a global aphasia, nothing medical—more like a transit strike of the intangible. You open your mouth and the air stays empty; words queue outside the picket line, brandishing signs that read PAY US IN SILENCE. How quickly would we revert to pointing, grunting, sketching charcoal bison on office walls? Would stock exchanges learn to read the twist of a wrist, the flare of a nostril? Couples who have said everything would discover fresh continents in the space between held hands. Or maybe we’d just shop by barcode, pay by blink, love by algorithmic heartbeat, letting numbers do what nouns used to.

But the strike won’t come; words are too entrepreneurial. They franchise themselves into new dialects, leap borders inside pop songs, disguised as choruses you can’t evict from your skull. They breed. Yesterday I invented “glumble”—a sullen rumble of thunder that sounds personally offended—and already I’ve overheard it twice, once from a barista describing her ex. The word is out there paying rent in someone else’s mouth, and I receive no royalties, only the vertigo of a parent spotting their kid on a stranger’s shoulders at a carnival.

Still, there are consolations. Last winter I took a night train through the Carpathians. No Wi-Fi, scant English, a cabin that smelled of burnt tea and pine. I shared the compartment with an elderly Romanian woman clutching a paper bag of walnuts. She spoke, I spoke; our languages failed to overlap, but our sentences hovered in the air like overlapping transparencies. She offered walnuts; I offered oranges. We invented a tiny pidgin of gestures: rotating fist for “cold,” flat palm drumming chest for “grateful.” Somewhere between stations, she began to recite what I later learned were folk rhymes. The rhythm was tide enough; I closed my eyes and heard Homer, heard hip-hop, heard my mother singing lullabies in a hospital corridor the night my son was born. Meaning had gone shy, yet cadence threw its arms around us. When she left at Brașov, she pressed a walnut shell into my palm. Inside was a rolled-up word neither of us had uttered—maybe the Romanian equivalent of “safe-travel,” maybe something larger, like “remember-me-although-we-will-never-solve-each-other.” The shell sits on my desk now, rattling faintly when trains pass at night.

I keep thinking that someday technology will let us taste languages, tongues interfacing tongues across continents. You’d lick your phone and I’d lick mine, and between us a flavor would bloom—miso-sweet for condolence, iron-edge for warning. Children would grow up bilingual in taste: they’d know before they could read that forgiveness is a hint of cardamom dissolving into warm bread. Maybe wars would stall because no one could stomach the bitterness required to declare one. Or maybe we’d simply develop a new elite of flavor editors, paid to spice corporate apologies until the public swallows anything.

Yet even then, something would escape. The library of lost syllables would annex a new wing of un-tasted notes, and the green-bakelite phone would ring with a recipe no tongue can follow: “Take one part fog, two parts echo, season with the pause your father took before answering the question you never dared ask.” The house would still creak; the walnut would still rattle; words would still change coats after midnight and slip back into daylight disguised as the everyday.

And I will keep trying to pay rent in them, not to own but to haunt, the way a scent haunts an elevator long after the wearer has stepped off. One day my voice will fail, but a sentence of mine—bent, reborn, unrecognizable—may find its way to the lips of a stranger waiting in line for coffee, and for a moment they’ll feel the floors of their chest rearrange, a tiny renovation. They won’t know my name; I won’t know theirs; yet something will have been repaired, or perhaps newly broken, which is sometimes the same gift wrapped in different paper.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
